text: The Talmudic tractate Avot de-Rabbi Natan elaborates on Rabbi Tarfon's teaching with a striking metaphor: the worker hired for the day who cannot complete the building before sunset. The worker's inability to finish is not failure — it is the natural structure of time. The rabbis used this image deliberately. They were not primarily consoling the discouraged; they were reframing what it means to show up. The building does not stand on the final stone; it stands on every stone laid faithfully. A distracted hour at the project's end contributes less than an attentive hour at its middle. The ethics are in the quality of participation, not its duration.
question: What does the day-laborer metaphor in Avot de-Rabbi Natan primarily illustrate?

options.text: That wages should be paid at the end of each workday
That natural time constraints excuse scholars from completing difficult texts
That the ethics lie in quality of participation, not its duration
That even simple labor contains spiritual significance when done humbly
